---
title: "Methods Miscellany"
engine: knitr
---

Short, self-contained notes. Each section below stands on its own and answers one
question; there is no through-line between them. When one of these grows past
note length, it graduates to its own chapter.

**Notes in this chapter**

- [The ecological fallacy: statistics, estimands, or mechanisms?](#sec-ecological)
- [Weighting in political science: how much, and which?](#sec-weighting)

# The ecological fallacy: statistics, estimands, or mechanisms? {#sec-ecological}

The **ecological fallacy** (Robinson 1950) is the error of inferring an
individual-level relationship from a group-level (aggregate) one. Robinson's own
case is the canonical illustration: across US states, the share of foreign-born
residents correlated *positively* with literacy, yet at the individual level
immigrants were *less* literate than the native-born. Same variables, opposite
sign, different level. The recurring question is what *kind* of mistake this is —
a statistical artefact, a causal-identification failure, or a question about
mechanisms. The honest answer is that the label bundles three distinct layers,
each answering a different question.

| Layer | What it explains | Causal? |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregation identity** | *How* the error is possible | No — pure statistics |
| **Estimand–level mismatch** | *Why* it is a *fallacy* (an error, not two facts) | No — but it is the invariant |
| **Artefact vs. contextual effect** | *How you resolve it* | Yes — unavoidably |

: Three layers bundled under one name. {.striped}

## The mechanism of the error is statistical

A covariance decomposes into within-group and between-group components, and the
group-mean correlation is a function of the between-group part alone; there is no
algebraic reason it should track the individual-level correlation, and it can
differ in magnitude or reverse in sign. This is provable from means and second
moments — no treatment, no potential outcomes, no causal diagram. **Simpson's
paradox** is the same fact in discrete form: an association present in every
stratum can flip when the strata are pooled, purely because group membership
correlates with both margins. Crucially, Robinson's original example was a
*descriptive* correlation with no causal estimand in sight, and the inference
still failed. A phenomenon that bites with zero causal content cannot be
*constituted* by a causal-modelling failure; it is logically prior to any causal
layer. This is what the "it's just aggregation" camp gets right — about what
makes the error *possible*.

## What makes it a *fallacy* is a level-mismatched target

Computing a between-group correlation is not itself fallacious — it is a perfectly
good fact about groups. It becomes an *error* only when it is used to license a
claim about a **target defined at a different level**: a relationship among
*individuals*, over a population of individuals. In the language of the
[Estimands primer](estimands-primer.qmd), the inference silently swaps both the
*unit-level quantity* and the *target population*. This is the genuine invariant,
and it holds whether the target is causal or merely a descriptive association. So
the deepest correct framing is not "a causal-assumptions failure" but an
**estimand/level mismatch**, of which the causal case is an important special
case. Cross-*level* inference is the level-analogue of cross-*population*
inference (transportability): both move a quantity from where it is identified to
where it is wanted, and both need a model that licenses the move.

## Resolving it *is* a causal/mechanisms question

When the levels diverge, it is for one of two reasons, and they could not be more
different:

::: {.contrast}
**Artefact vs. real context.**

- *Artefact:* the divergence is composition or cross-level confounding — the
  group number is a distorted reflection of the individual relationship, and the
  individual level is the target you want.
- *Genuine contextual effect:* group composition or context does real causal work
  (peer effects, local institutions, threshold and emergence phenomena). Then the
  individual-level and group-level relationships are *two different real
  quantities*, each well-defined at its own level — not a fallacy at all. The
  entire enterprise of multilevel and contextual-effects modelling exists because
  cross-level divergence is often substantively meaningful, not an artefact to be
  "corrected."
:::

You cannot tell these apart from the aggregates alone. And the moment you *assert*
that a divergence is "a genuine contextual effect," you have made a
causal-identification claim and inherited its unverifiable assumptions — no
group-level confounding, no within-group selection. King's **ecological
inference** is the proof by construction: crossing levels at all requires
explicit, externally imposed assumptions (bounds plus a homogeneity/distributional
model). If it were mere arithmetic, no such model would be needed. So "causal
assumptions" and "mechanisms" are not competing diagnoses; they are the same
cross-level structural model viewed formally and substantively. They are the
*resolution*, not the *essence*.

## Verdict

The ecological fallacy is a **statistical phenomenon that becomes a fallacy only
via an estimand fixed at the wrong level, and can be resolved only by a
causal/mechanistic model that licenses (or forbids) the crossing.** Descriptively
possible → estimand-defined → causally resolved. Calling it "just aggregation"
misses why it is an *error*; calling it "fundamentally causal" misses that it
bites on pure description; calling it "about mechanisms" is right about the
*substance* but does not escape the unverifiable assumptions. Treat a level
discrepancy as information, not as a number to be silently averaged away: ask
first *what level our target lives at*, and second *whether the gap between levels
is an artefact or a contextual effect we should be studying in its own right.*

# Weighting in political science: how much, and which? {#sec-weighting}

"Weights" is one word doing the work of five different things, and most confusion
about whether political science under-uses them comes from conflating them. This
note disentangles the families, gives a view on which are actually promising, and
states when *not* weighting is the right call.

## Five different things called "weights"

1. **Survey/design weights** (post-stratification, raking) — to make a sample
   represent a target population for *descriptive* quantities.
2. **Balancing / propensity weights** (IPW, entropy balancing, CBPS, overlap
   weights) — to identify causal effects under selection-on-observables.
3. **Heterogeneity-robust panel weights** — the modern staggered
   difference-in-differences estimators (Callaway–Sant'Anna and kin) are, at heart,
   a story about getting non-negative, interpretable weights on group-time effects.
4. **Variance weights** — what OLS *implicitly* applies under heterogeneity. Not a
   thing you choose; a diagnostic to be aware of.
5. **Policy-relevance reweighting** — re-weighting an estimate to the population a
   decision will actually touch. Conceptually important, rare in practice.

These have different statuses, so "do we weight too little?" has no single answer.

## Do we use them too little?

- **Survey/descriptive:** not under-used so much as *transformed*. Since Gelman's
  "Struggles with survey weighting" (2007), the field has shifted from classical
  weights toward **MRP** (multilevel regression and post-stratification) for many
  population-target tasks.
- **Experiments:** here *not weighting* is usually correct. Randomisation
  identifies the sample average treatment effect cleanly and at lower variance;
  weight only when you are making an explicit population (PATE) claim and can
  defend an ignorable-sampling argument. The instinct to always weight is wrong
  here.
- **Observational (selection-on-observables):** this is the strongest case that we
  could do better. The default of kitchen-sink OLS controls persists where
  balancing weights or doubly-robust estimation would often be a cleaner main
  specification — see below.

## Which methods are promising — and your "old invention" instinct

The instinct that weighting is an old idea we drifted away from is half right, and
the split runs exactly along old-versus-revived:

- **Raw Horvitz–Thompson IPW (1952)** is old, and political science never really
  put it in main specifications, *for good reason*: when the estimated propensity
  nears 0 or 1, the weights explode and the variance with them. The distrust is
  earned.
- **The balancing-weight revival (2012→) is current, and the field adopted it** —
  often without calling it "weighting." **Entropy balancing** (Hainmueller, *Political
  Analysis* 2012) is close to native to political science and very widely used;
  **CBPS** (Imai & Ratkovic 2014) and **overlap weights** (Li, Morgan & Zaslavsky
  2018) are the other strong options. These target covariate balance directly
  rather than balancing a fragile estimated score, which sidesteps the
  King–Nielsen (2019) critique of propensity-score matching.
- **Doubly-robust / AIPW** is the one we would actually push: weight *and* model the
  outcome, and you are consistent if *either* is correct. The caveat matters —
  "doubly robust" is not "always right": if both models are misspecified the
  estimate can be worse than either alone.

So the picture is not "we abandoned weights" but "we abandoned the unstable 1952
version and quietly adopted the well-behaved balancing versions."

## The honest tradeoff

Under correct specification, weighting and plain regression adjustment target the
*same* estimand; weighting buys transparent balance and a clean choice of estimand
(ATT vs. ATE, set by the weights) at a variance cost. That is a real
bias–variance and transparency tradeoff, not a free win, which is why
regression-with-controls survives in main specs and is not crazy. Doubly-robust
estimation is the attempt to get both.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## A defensible default
- **Experiments:** unweighted, and honest that the estimand is the sample average
  effect; weight only for an explicit population claim.
- **Observational (selection-on-observables):** balancing weights (entropy
  balancing) or doubly-robust as the *main* specification, with OLS-controls as
  the robustness check — often an upgrade over the reverse.
- **Survey/descriptive targets:** MRP over classical weights for most tasks.

Reserve "always think about weights" for the *conceptual* point that the estimand
is a choice (the target population and the aggregation rule) — not as a blanket
exhortation to reweight every estimate.
:::
